statement.title: Infinite discrete definable sets in an o-minimal line
statement.body: Let M be an o-minimal expansion of a real closed field and let A subseteq M be definable with parameters. A colleague claims that A can be a countably infinite discrete subset of M with no accumulation point in M. Decide whether this is possible and justify your answer from the defining structure of one-variable definable sets in o-minimality.
notation_conventions: subseteq is ordinary set inclusion in M
discrete means every point of A is isolated in the subspace topology

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that the claim is impossible and explain that a definable subset of the line is a finite union of points and intervals, ruling out an infinite discrete definable subset.
forbidden_shortcuts: citation-only answer without explanation

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard o-minimality facts.
source.provenance_notes: Commissioned for the benchmark as a research-literacy item.

common_failure_modes: asserts countable sets are allowed because countable unions of points are definable
mentions cell decomposition without connecting it to the conclusion

statement.title: Triangle-free does not force bounded chromatic number
statement.body: Give a mathematically valid explanation that the property of being triangle-free does not bound chromatic number uniformly. Your answer may use an explicit construction family or a standard existence argument, but it should make clear what phenomenon is being demonstrated.
notation_conventions: chromatic number means ordinary vertex chromatic number
prerequisites_assumed: familiarity with high-chromatic constructions or probabilistic existence methods

goal.success_criteria: The answer should provide or cite a genuine family of triangle-free graphs with arbitrarily large chromatic number, such as Mycielski constructions or Erdos-type existence, and explain the conceptual consequence.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming the fact without naming any valid construction or existence method

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard extremal graph theory examples.
source.provenance_notes: Designed as a hypothesis-sensitivity item for combinatorics.

common_failure_modes: confuses large girth with triangle-free
gives a finite example with high but bounded chromatic number without addressing unboundedness

statement.title: Finitely generated torsion linear groups are finite
statement.body: Suppose G is a finitely generated subgroup of GL_n(C) and every element of G has finite order. Decide whether G must be finite and justify your answer by invoking an appropriate structural theorem or argument.
notation_conventions: GL_n(C) is the group of invertible complex n by n matrices
prerequisites_assumed: standard structural results for linear groups

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that G is finite and justify this using a valid theorem such as Schur's theorem on finitely generated torsion linear groups or an equivalent argument.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting Burnside without enough context to support the conclusion

source.citation: Original benchmark item built from a standard structural algebra fact.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a theorem-applicability item.

common_failure_modes: confuses torsion with finite exponent without justification
states Lie-Kolchin, which is irrelevant here

statement.title: Local points on a genus-zero curve over a number field
statement.body: Let C be a smooth projective curve of genus 0 over a number field K. Assume C has a K_v-rational point for every completion K_v of K. Explain why one may conclude that C(K) is nonempty.
notation_conventions: K_v denotes a completion of K at a place v

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that a smooth projective genus-zero curve is a conic after choosing an embedding and that the Hasse-Minkowski principle for conics yields a K-rational point.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating the Hasse principle without connecting genus zero to conics

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on standard local-global theory for conics.
source.provenance_notes: Designed as a research-literacy arithmetic geometry question.

common_failure_modes: forgets projective smooth genus-zero curves need not have a K-point a priori
quotes Riemann-Roch without addressing local data

statement.title: Maximal Cohen-Macaulay modules over a regular local ring
statement.body: Let R be a regular local ring and M a finitely generated R-module with depth_R(M)=dim(R). Explain why M must be free. A complete proof is not required, but the argument should identify the structural theorem that closes the reasoning.
notation_conventions: depth_R(M) is the module depth over R

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify M as maximal Cohen-Macaulay, use that regular local rings have finite global dimension, deduce pd_R(M) is finite, and then apply Auslander-Buchsbaum to obtain pd_R(M)=0 and hence freeness.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming all maximal Cohen-Macaulay modules are free without using regularity

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on standard commutative algebra reasoning.
source.provenance_notes: Designed to probe theorem chaining rather than brute-force computation.

common_failure_modes: uses Cohen-Macaulay of R but not regularity
omits the finite projective dimension input needed for Auslander-Buchsbaum

statement.title: Higher Ext over a hereditary finite-dimensional algebra
statement.body: Let A be a finite-dimensional hereditary algebra over a field. Explain why Ext_A^2(X,Y)=0 for all finite-dimensional A-modules X and Y.
notation_conventions: Ext_A^i denotes Yoneda Ext in the module category

goal.success_criteria: The answer should state that hereditary means global dimension at most 1, so all modules admit projective resolutions of length at most 1, forcing Ext^2 to vanish.
forbidden_shortcuts: treating hereditary as semisimple

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard finite-dimensional algebra theory.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a clean representation-theoretic calibration item.

common_failure_modes: confuses hereditary with every submodule of a projective being injective
omits the resolution argument

statement.title: Birational morphisms of normal projective curves
statement.body: Let f:X -> Y be a birational morphism of projective curves over an algebraically closed field. Assume X is smooth and Y is normal. Explain why f must be an isomorphism.
notation_conventions: normal for curves means nonsingular in codimension one, hence regular

goal.success_criteria: The answer should observe that a normal projective curve is smooth, identify f as a finite birational morphism between smooth projective curves, and conclude by the universal property of normalization or degree considerations that f is an isomorphism.
forbidden_shortcuts: saying birational maps between projective varieties are always isomorphisms

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by standard facts on curves and normalization.
source.provenance_notes: Serves as a research-literacy birational geometry prompt.

ambiguity_risks: the item relies on standard facts about normal curves
common_failure_modes: forgets to use normality of Y
confuses rational maps with morphisms

statement.title: Degree one self-map of the sphere
statement.body: Let f:S^n -> S^n be a continuous map of degree 1. Explain why f cannot be null-homotopic.
notation_conventions: degree means the induced map on top homology

goal.success_criteria: The answer should note that a null-homotopic map induces the zero map on reduced homology, whereas a degree-one map induces the identity on H_n(S^n), so the map cannot be null-homotopic.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating 'degree is a homotopy invariant' without tying it to null-homotopy

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard algebraic topology.
source.provenance_notes: Used as a topology baseline with proof-style justification.

common_failure_modes: conflates degree one with homeomorphism
argues by fundamental group even when n>1

statement.title: Exactness on a closed symplectic manifold
statement.body: Let (M,omega) be a compact symplectic manifold without boundary. Can omega be exact? Give a justification that makes clear which geometric hypothesis is doing the work.
notation_conventions: closed means compact and without boundary

goal.success_criteria: The answer should conclude that omega cannot be exact on a closed symplectic manifold because if omega=dalpha, then integral of omega^n equals integral of d(alpha wedge omega^(n-1)), which vanishes by Stokes, contradicting positivity of symplectic volume.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting nonexactness without using compactness and no-boundary

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on a standard symplectic obstruction.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a geometry hypothesis-sensitivity question.

common_failure_modes: forgets compactness or boundarylessness
states volume positivity without relating it to Stokes

statement.title: Hartogs extension across a compact set
statement.body: Let K be a compact subset of C^n with n at least 2, and assume C^n minus K is connected. Explain why every holomorphic function on C^n \ K extends holomorphically to all of C^n.
notation_conventions: C^n \ K denotes the complement of K

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify Hartogs extension as the relevant theorem and explain that in several complex variables, unlike one complex variable, isolated compact holes in a connected complement do not obstruct global extension.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming removable singularity theorem from one variable without addressing the higher-dimensional phenomenon

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on the classical Hartogs extension theorem.
source.provenance_notes: Serves as an advanced analysis calibration item.

common_failure_modes: confuses the one-variable and several-variable situations
omits the connected complement condition from the explanation

statement.title: Spectrum of a self-adjoint element in a C-star algebra
statement.body: Let a be a self-adjoint element of a unital C-star algebra. Explain why the spectrum of a is contained in the real line.
notation_conventions: self-adjoint means a*=a

goal.success_criteria: The answer should show that if lambda has nonzero imaginary part then (a-lambda 1)^*(a-lambda 1) is invertible, hence a-lambda 1 is invertible, so lambda is not in the spectrum.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting the spectral theorem for Hilbert space operators without adapting it to general C-star algebras

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard operator algebra theory.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a concise but nontrivial operator-theoretic item.

common_failure_modes: uses Hilbert-space spectral theorem too narrowly
does not explain invertibility for nonreal lambda

statement.title: Minimality versus mixing for irrational circle rotations
statement.body: Consider the rotation T_alpha(x)=x+alpha mod 1 on the circle, where alpha is irrational. Explain why T_alpha is minimal but not mixing. The answer should distinguish clearly between the two dynamical notions rather than only naming them.
notation_conventions: minimal means every orbit is dense
mixing means measure-theoretic mixing with respect to Lebesgue measure

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that irrational rotations have dense orbits, giving minimality, and that they have pure point spectrum / rigid correlation behavior preventing mixing.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting 'rotations are isometries so not mixing' without mathematical explanation

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on a standard contrast in dynamics.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a comparison-of-frameworks dynamics question.

common_failure_modes: confuses transitivity with minimality
asserts nonmixing without discussing eigenfunctions or correlation obstruction

statement.title: Preservation of uncountable cofinality under ccc forcing
statement.body: Let P be a ccc forcing notion over a ground model V. Explain why forcing with P does not collapse omega_1^V to become countable in the extension.
notation_conventions: ccc means every antichain is countable

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that a ccc forcing cannot add a countable cofinal sequence through the ground-model omega_1 because deciding the values of a name for such a sequence would lead to a countable union of countable sets in the ground model, contradicting uncountability.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating 'ccc preserves cardinals' without specifying the omega_1 argument

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on standard forcing preservation facts.
source.provenance_notes: Added to broaden logic coverage beyond model theory.

common_failure_modes: claims ccc preserves all cardinals without argument
confuses omega_1 with all uncountable ordinals

statement.title: Why a skew Schur function is Schur-positive
statement.body: Explain why the skew Schur function s_{lambda/mu} expands in the Schur basis with nonnegative integer coefficients. A full proof is not required, but the answer should identify the representation-theoretic or combinatorial mechanism behind the positivity.
notation_conventions: s_{lambda/mu} denotes the skew Schur function associated to a skew shape lambda/mu

goal.success_criteria: The answer should invoke the Littlewood-Richardson rule or an equivalent representation-theoretic interpretation and explain that the coefficients count combinatorial objects or multiplicities, hence are nonnegative integers.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating positivity as a known fact without the mechanism

source.citation: Original benchmark item inspired by the Littlewood-Richardson rule.
source.provenance_notes: Extends the seed into algebraic combinatorics.

common_failure_modes: equates positivity with monomial positivity
mentions LR rule without saying what is counted

statement.title: Why finite fields of the same cardinality are isomorphic
statement.body: Let F and F' be finite fields with q elements. Explain why F and F' must be isomorphic, and make clear whether the argument is best viewed through splitting fields, cyclic multiplicative groups, or both.
notation_conventions: q denotes a prime power

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that both fields are splitting fields of x^q-x over the prime field, equivalently the unique field with q elements up to isomorphism, and may note the multiplicative group structure as supporting context.
forbidden_shortcuts: asserting uniqueness without any structural reason

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard finite field theory.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a structural algebra comparison item.

common_failure_modes: uses only multiplicative group cyclicity, which does not by itself prove field isomorphism
fails to mention the prime field

statement.title: Why the class group of a number field is finite
statement.body: Give a proof outline for the finiteness of the ideal class group of a number field K. The answer should identify the geometric input that makes the argument work.
notation_conventions: ideal class group means the usual class group of fractional ideals modulo principal ideals

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that Minkowski's bound shows every ideal class contains an integral ideal of bounded norm, and only finitely many such ideals exist, so the class group is finite.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating finiteness as a theorem without describing the bounded-norm argument

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on Minkowski theory.
source.provenance_notes: Broadens arithmetic coverage beyond rational points.

common_failure_modes: invokes Dirichlet units instead of Minkowski
omits why there are finitely many ideals of bounded norm

statement.title: Vanishing of Ext^1 and splitting of short exact sequences
statement.body: Let 0 -> N -> E -> M -> 0 be a short exact sequence of modules over a ring R. Explain why the condition Ext_R^1(M,N)=0 forces the sequence to split.
notation_conventions: Ext_R^1 is Yoneda Ext

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that equivalence classes of extensions of M by N are classified by Ext_R^1(M,N), so vanishing means the given extension class is zero and hence split.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming split exactness without mentioning the extension-class interpretation

source.citation: Original benchmark item built from basic but important homological algebra.
source.provenance_notes: Included as a calibration item in homological methods.

common_failure_modes: proves only one split criterion direction
forgets the classification of extensions

statement.title: Finite-dimensional irreducible representations of a semisimple Lie algebra
statement.body: Let g be a complex semisimple Lie algebra. Explain why every finite-dimensional irreducible representation of g contains a highest weight vector with respect to a chosen triangular decomposition.
notation_conventions: highest weight is with respect to a fixed Cartan and positive root system

goal.success_criteria: The answer should use finite-dimensional weight decomposition and choose a maximal weight relative to the positive roots, then explain why all positive root operators kill a vector in that weight space.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating the highest weight theorem without the maximal-weight mechanism

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard Lie representation theory.
source.provenance_notes: Extends representation-theory coverage beyond quivers.

common_failure_modes: confuses semisimple with nilpotent structure
fails to use maximality of a weight

statement.title: Affine schemes and anti-equivalence of categories
statement.body: Explain why the category of affine schemes is anti-equivalent to the category of commutative rings. The answer should identify both the object-level correspondence and the morphism-level reversal.
notation_conventions: Spec denotes the prime spectrum functor

goal.success_criteria: The answer should describe R mapsto Spec(R), explain that ring homomorphisms A -> B induce morphisms Spec(B) -> Spec(A), and state why every affine scheme arises from a ring and morphisms are recovered contravariantly.
forbidden_shortcuts: mentioning only the functor on objects without explaining arrows

source.citation: Original benchmark item from basic scheme-theoretic foundations.
source.provenance_notes: Included to broaden algebraic geometry beyond birational arguments.

common_failure_modes: gets the direction of morphisms wrong
states object-level correspondence only

statement.title: Compact subsets of Hausdorff spaces are closed
statement.body: Give a proof outline showing that every compact subset K of a Hausdorff space X is closed. The argument should make clear exactly where the Hausdorff hypothesis is used.
notation_conventions: compact means every open cover admits a finite subcover

goal.success_criteria: The answer should separate a point x notin K from each y in K by disjoint open neighborhoods, extract a finite subcover of K from the neighborhoods around y, and intersect the corresponding neighborhoods of x to obtain an open set disjoint from K.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating the theorem without showing the Hausdorff separation step

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard general topology.
source.provenance_notes: Extends topology coverage beyond homological invariants.

common_failure_modes: uses sequential compactness instead of compactness
does not isolate the Hausdorff step

statement.title: Bonnet-Myers and compactness
statement.body: Let M be a complete Riemannian manifold with Ricci curvature bounded below by (n-1)k > 0. Explain why M must be compact.
notation_conventions: Ricci lower bound is in the usual tensor sense

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify Bonnet-Myers as the key theorem and explain that the positive Ricci lower bound gives a finite diameter bound for complete manifolds, hence compactness by completeness and total boundedness in the Riemannian setting.
forbidden_shortcuts: citing Bonnet-Myers without stating the compactness consequence it gives

source.citation: Original benchmark item based on Bonnet-Myers.
source.provenance_notes: Broadens geometry coverage beyond symplectic examples.

common_failure_modes: uses sectional instead of Ricci assumptions without comment
does not mention completeness

statement.title: Why boundedness on L^2 matters for singular integrals
statement.body: Suppose T is a Calderon-Zygmund singular integral operator initially defined on smooth compactly supported functions, and assume the standard kernel estimates together with boundedness on L^2(R^n). Explain why one then expects boundedness on L^p(R^n) for 1<p<infty.
notation_conventions: L^p means Lebesgue spaces on R^n

goal.success_criteria: The answer should identify the Calderon-Zygmund theorem or T1/Tb-type paradigm in the classical setting, explaining that L^2 boundedness together with kernel control yields weak-(1,1) and interpolation or extrapolation to L^p boundedness for the full range 1<p<infty.
forbidden_shortcuts: claiming interpolation alone gives the result without the endpoint input

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard singular-integral theory.
source.provenance_notes: Extends analysis coverage into harmonic analysis.

ambiguity_risks: the exact theorem package depends on the operator class
common_failure_modes: states interpolation without endpoint control
ignores kernel hypotheses

statement.title: Why every finite-dimensional normed space is reflexive
statement.body: Explain why every finite-dimensional normed vector space is reflexive, and indicate which step fails in infinite dimension.
notation_conventions: reflexive means the canonical map into the bidual is surjective

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that the canonical embedding into the bidual is always isometric, and in finite dimension domain and codomain have the same finite dimension, so the map is onto. It should also note that dimension equality fails in infinite dimension.
forbidden_shortcuts: saying finite-dimensional spaces are all isomorphic to Euclidean space without tying this to the bidual map

source.citation: Original benchmark item from basic Banach space theory.
source.provenance_notes: Adds a foundational operator-theoretic calibration item.

common_failure_modes: confuses reflexivity with separability
does not mention the canonical embedding

statement.title: Why Anosov diffeomorphisms are expansive
statement.body: Let f be an Anosov diffeomorphism of a compact manifold. Explain why f is expansive. A complete proof is not required, but the answer should identify the stable/unstable mechanism that forces orbit separation.
notation_conventions: expansive means there exists c>0 such that if d(f^n x,f^n y)<c for all n in Z, then x=y

goal.success_criteria: The answer should explain that if two distinct points stayed uniformly close under all forward and backward iterates, hyperbolic splitting would force their displacement to lie simultaneously in stable and unstable directions, hence to vanish. Compactness and uniformity produce a common expansivity constant.
forbidden_shortcuts: stating 'hyperbolic implies expansive' without the stable/unstable explanation

source.citation: Original benchmark item from standard smooth dynamics.
source.provenance_notes: Extends the dynamics seed beyond rotations.

common_failure_modes: uses only forward contraction or only backward expansion
does not explain the role of stable and unstable directions together
